text: Welcome to another episode of robotic hernia surgery. Today, we're going to review a recent operation I performed on a patient, an ETEP unilateral transversus abdominis release. This is a fifty-two year old man with an incarcerated recurrent ventral hernia. He had previously presented with a concern for strangulation under emergency exploratory laparotomy with primary hernia repair. I optimized him for some time. He lost some weight. He quit smoking, and he had his diabetes much better controlled. And then we planned an operation. Cross-sectional imaging was obtained per usual with a hernia that measured about nine by twelve. He was a very large man. He was a retro rectus width on either side of nearly fourteen centimeters. So I thought that ETEP would be a great option for him.

The surgeon applies a left retromuscular access using an ETEP approach, inserting the applied medical first entry trocar to insufflate the area while the inner cannula is in place. The trocar punches through the muscle, allowing entry into the retromuscular space for pneumodissection. Ports are positioned near the semilunar line, with the entry trocar centrally placed in the rectus muscle. The posterior sheath is incised on the patient's left side, approximately one centimeter from the linea alba, entering the preperitoneal fat pad. The surgeon clears the peritoneum to prevent injury to underlying viscera and performs contralateral retro rectus dissection.

The surgeon develops the retromuscular space before addressing the hernia, focusing on the cephalic component where incarcerated omentum and bowel are in close proximity. The hernia sac appears thin, prompting an incision into it to release the viscera. The surgeon extracts omentum and other tissues from multiple hernia sacs over the course of the operation.

The surgeon identifies defects and works to develop a space, noting a large posterior hole due to a thin hernia sac. The posterior sheath is mobilized, and insufflation is confirmed for adequate retro rectus dissection. The surgeon incises the posterior lamella of the internal abdominal oblique medial to the perforators, exposing and transecting the transversus abdominis, progressing to the pre-transversal space.

The surgeon uses cautery and dissection to mobilize the posterior sheath away from the muscle, preserving the transversalis fascia with the peritoneum. The dissection progresses pre-transversalis, revealing the retroperitoneal fat pad and arcuate line. The transversalis fascia is incised, and the posterior sheath is completely mobilized, resulting in a lax posterior. The anterior sheath is visible, and the surgeon closes the posterior sheath first.

The surgeon demonstrates suturing techniques for closing the posterior sheath, using a baseball-style stitch to carefully re-approximate the edges. A thorough inspection is conducted to identify and close any holes in the peritoneum before proceeding with anterior fascial defect closure. The fascia and diastasis are placated as the surgeon approaches the hernia site.

The surgeon uses a permanent V-lock suture on a GS-XXI, number one, to sequentially tighten and re-approximate the fascia around the multifocal hernia. The suturing technique involves a double pulley system, with the left hand moving up and the right hand moving down, facilitating efficient closure. The V-lock suture is locked, and the surgeon prepares to place a thirty by thirty polypropylene mesh.

The surgeon positions a mesh in a diamond orientation, measuring up to forty-three centimeters in length, but uses only thirty-eight centimeters. The mesh is secured to the tissue, and due to the bloodless dissection, no drain is placed. The procedure concludes with a same-day discharge for the patient.
